text: The surgeon begins the intervention by controlling the uterine vessels at their origin using a medial approach, preparing to manage potential hemorrhage during the myomectomy.
The surgeon begins the procedure by opening the posterior phase of the broad ligaments, preparing for the medial control of the uterine vessels.
The surgeon introduces gas into the large ligament, creating a 'champagne effect' that expands the ligament, facilitating the identification and dissection of the ureter.

The video shows two contiguous conduits in fuselage: an external vascular conduit and an internal urinary conduit. The retro-ligamentary portion of the urethra is visible, running parallel to the inner iliac artery.
The surgeon demonstrates the rotation of the urethra, gradually detaching it from the inner iliac artery, highlighting the spatial relationship between these structures.
The surgeon performs slow, progressive movements to reveal a vessel detaching medially from the internal iliac artery, identified as the uterine artery.

The surgeon identifies the internal iliac artery, with the uterus visible inside the scissor. As the dissection progresses from the caudal side, the uterine artery is located. The urethra is clearly visible and crosses the uterine artery at a certain point. The vessel detaching from the inner iliac artery is observed pulsating near the ureter.
The surgeon opens the left broad ligament while the assistant holds the scissors. The surgeon focuses on treating the cranial aspect of the adnexa to expose the posterior phase of the broad ligament.

The surgeon locates the ureter, identifying two lateral contiguous conduits in the gun barrel view. The internal conduit corresponds to the ureter, while the external conduit is identified as the internal iliac artery.

The surgeon suspects the identified vessel is the internal arteriole and checks its identity by moving backwards to locate the vessel external to the uterine artery, which corresponds to the umbilical artery. The surgeon pulls on the umbilical artery to confirm its identity at the abdominal level before proceeding to clip the uterine artery.

The surgeon holds the umbilical artery, visually confirming its identity before proceeding. The uterine artery is identified as the vessel branching from the umbilical artery. The ureter is visible and under control, while the surgeon prepares to clip the uterine artery at a slightly lower position than usual.
The uterine vein is visible below the surgical field, positioned beneath the uterine artery and adjacent to the ureter.

The surgeon performs a myomectomy using a bipolar band and scissors, making a vertical incision on the uterus that spans approximately sixty to seventy percent of its volume. The fibroma being removed measures about seventy millimeters, and the incision is approximately five centimeters long.

The surgeon uses scissors to treat the fibrous tracts binding the fibroma to the uterus, applying coagulation on the fibroma while avoiding the myometrium. The fibroma is carefully extracted from the myometrium without using energy, with frequent adjustments to the position of the pinch for effective traction.
The surgeon coagulates the tissues attached to the fibroma capsule, followed by rapid coagulation of the fibroma itself against the uterus. Hemostasis is achieved quickly using sutures.

The surgeon uses Bicryl No. I and Gumil-Carran sutures to create large holds, effectively invaginating the hysterotomy scar. This technique includes part of the myometrium within the fibrous crater, aiming to reduce the risk of hematoma formation in the fibrous bed.

The surgeon performs suturing on the posterior phase of the uterus, creating a fully peritoneal hysterotomy scar to reduce the risk of postoperative adhesion. Atracorporeal nodes are used for better tightening and adjustment, with the thread being blocked immediately to complete the sutures.

The surgeon places sutures deep within the uterine tissue adjacent to the hysterotomy scar, using two or three points to secure the area around the large fibroma. The sutures are positioned to minimize the number of points needed for effective closure.

The surgeon carefully makes a single entrance and exit with the suture needle to minimize bleeding and prevent weakening of the myometrial wall. The suture is adapted to the remaining volume or distance on the biometer, ensuring preservation of the uterine ligament and synaptic connections between the ovary and trunk, as the patient desires fertility.
The surgeon tightens the suture thread with assistance, completing the suturing process smoothly without any visible complications.

The surgeon places a final stitch to secure the top of the pleat, carefully inserting the needle in the opposite direction to avoid piercing the right and left tubular ostia, ensuring the preservation of fertility.

The surgeon chisels the fibroma, placing the chisel in the left foscilia, adjusted to eight hundred turns per minute. The fibroma is prevented from shining to avoid dispersing tissue into the pelvis. The ureter is checked, showing it is free and passes under the clipped uterine artery. The contralateral side is checked similarly, confirming the uterine artery is clipped and the clips remain in place. The uterus is visible inside, while the uterine artery is outside, and the area is washed.
